title: Augmented Journeys: Interactive Points of Interest for In-Car Augmented Reality
description: A prototype system enabling rear-seat passengers to explore their surroundings via world-fixed Points of Interest displayed in AR. Three visualization techniques (Timeline, Minimap, List) were designed and evaluated. Published at CHI 2025.

title: Blending the Worlds: POI Visualization in Automotive AR
description: A field study (N=38) examining six parameters for visualizing world-fixed POIs via a pass-through HMD in a moving vehicle: positioning, scaling, rotation, render distance, information density, and appearance. Derived UX guidelines for in-car AR design.

title: AR Selection Techniques for In-Car Passengers
description: A real-world user study comparing four AR selection techniques for passengers in moving vehicles: head-gaze/dwell, head-gaze/button, eye-gaze/button, and hand pointing. Conducted with N=22 participants; eye gaze was preferred and performed on par with other methods.

title: Visual SLAM in Non-Stationary Environments
description: Part of the perceive(IT) Jahrestagung at Reutlingen University. I was in the organizing team handling reviews and logo design, and contributed a paper on Visual SLAM in non-stationary environments.

title: ViRGOS Missions
description: Student project at the VR Lab at Reutlingen University. The project replicates the International Space Station in Unity and includes interactive mission scenarios for immersive exploration and task-based interaction. We build a novel weightless locomotion system and evaluated it in a user study.

title: AR Drive Press Release
description: A public showcase at Mercedes-Benz highlighting work around their ambitions for in-car augmented reality. I was part of the project team that contributed to the prototype development.

title: Bachelor Thesis: Comparison of Inverse Kinematics and Motion Tracking in a VR Application
description: Bachelor thesis at Reutlingen University comparing inverse kinematics and motion tracking within a VR application. The work analyzes implementation effort and user experience trade-offs, including precision, immersion, and interaction quality across both approaches.

title: Master Thesis: Development and Simulation of an LT-SLAM System
description: Master thesis focused on the development and simulation of an LT-SLAM system. The work covers system design, implementation, and experimental simulation to evaluate localization and mapping performance in dynamic scenarios.

title: Trading Cards Feature Matching
description: Student project focused on detecting and identifying Magic: The Gathering cards using OpenCV feature matching. I built a pipeline for keypoint extraction, descriptor comparison, and robust card recognition under varying camera angles and lighting conditions.
